---
title: "Identifying Different Types of Alcohol Consumers in Germany: A Latent Class Analysis (LCA) Approach"
subtitle: "Institut für Statistik, München"
author: "Sinan Klein, Venüs Kocabas, Uzay Erturan, Nika Curcija, Alp Erdem"
date: "11 December 2025"
institute: "Betreuerin:Sabine Hoffmann"
format: 
  revealjs:
    css: customstyle.css
    slide-number: true
    transition: none
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    logo: "LMU_Muenchen_Logo.svg.png"
fontsize: 20pt
mainfont: "Arial"
color: "#003366"
---

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(haven)
library(cluster)
```
```{r setup, include=FALSE}
source("/Users/macbook/Desktop/LCA.R") 
```
## Agenda

-   **Introduction**
-   **Background and Terminology**
-   **Data Pre-Processing and Variable Selection**
-   **Identifying and Validating Latent Classes**
-   **Discussion**



## Introduction (1)

- [Data Set]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}:
    - German health & substance-use survey: 
        -  Sub-sample of data from the [Epidemiologischer Suchtsurvey (ESA) 2024]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}
      conducted by IFT Institut für Therapieforschung and Ipsos GmbH on behalf of the BMG
    - Nationwide standardized study:
        - focusing on alcohol, tobacco, medications, and illicit drug use in Germany
    - Cross-sectional design:
        - voluntary participation, standardized questionnaire, GDPR-compliant data handling   
    - Sample includes adults across a broad age range:
        - Online survey with [2,784 respondents]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"} (aged [16–85 years]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}) covering diverse sociodemographic groups

## Introduction (2)

![](plot88.png) 

## Introduction (3)

- [Why Is Identifying Alcohol Consumer Types Important?]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}

    - Helps understand [risk patterns and harmful drinking behaviours]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}
    - Supports targeted [public health interventions]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}
    - Reveals distinct groups with [different motivations and consumption profiles]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}
    - Enables better policy decisions on [prevention, taxation, and education]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}

- [Objectives of the Project]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}

    1. What [consumer types (latent classes)]{style="color:#006400;font-weight:bold;"} exist in German alcohol use, and how are they [identified through Latent Class Analysis (LCA)]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}? 

    2. Do these [latent classes]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"} differ, in terms of [age, gender,drinking frequency, and other key variables]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}? 

    3. How common are [the identified classes]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}, and how do they [vary in consumption intensity or risk factors]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}?

## Background and Terminology (1)

- [What is Latent Class Analysis (LCA)?]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}  

    - A [model-based clustering technique]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"} that identifies hidden subgroups (“latent classes”) based on response patterns..
    
    - Estimates [probabilistic class memberships]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"} for each individual.

    - Classes are defined by [item-response probabilities]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}.

    - Assumes [locally independent]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}
    - Well-suited for behavioral and psychological data, including alcohol-use typologies.

## Background and Terminology (2)

- [Structure of the LCA Model]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}:
    - [Class proportions]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}: Probability of belonging to each latent class.
    - [Item-response probabilities]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}: Probability of each variable outcome given a class.
    - [Local independence]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}: Once the latent class is known, all indicators become independent.
    
- [Estimation Method]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}:
    - Estimated via [Maximum Likelihood]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}, typically using the [EM algorithm]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}.
    - The model outputs:
        - Posterior class membership probabilities,
        - Class-specific item-response profiles,
        - Overall model fit criteria (BIC).

## Data Pre-Processing and Variable Selection (1)

- [Handling Missing Data (MAR)]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}:
    - [What is Missing At Random (MAR)?]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}
        - Missingness depends [only on observed variables]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}, not on the unobserved value itself.
        - MAR is valid for [likelihood-based LCA models]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}.
    - [How We Handled MAR in Our Analysis]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}  
        - Removed variables with excessive missingness to avoid unstable estimation.
        - Remaining missing values were handled automatically by the LCA likelihood estimation
    

## Data Pre-Processing and Variable Selection (2)

- [Variable Selection for Clustering]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}:
    - Variables Used for Clustering: 
        - Consumption risk category
        - DSM symptom indicators (binary)
        - Binge-drinking days (30-day standardized)
        - Severity score (alcohol-related consequences, e.g., police incidents, health or social problems)
    - Sociodemographic Variables for Post-Class Comparison:
        - Age
        - Gender
        - Income
        - Education


## Identifying and Validating Latent Classes (1)

- [Choosing the Number of Classes]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}:
    - LCA is estimated for several candidate class solutions (e.g., 2–5 classes).
    - Model selection typically relies on the[Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC)]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}.
        - [Lower BIC = better balance]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"} of model fit and simplicity.
        - BIC tends to favour stable, non-overfitted solutions.
    - After selecting the model with the lowest BIC, the classes must still be interpretable in the context of alcohol-use patterns.
    - [Final Choice]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}: The solution that combines [lowest BIC]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"} with [clear, meaningful class distinctions]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}.
    

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="65%"}
```{r}
lca_best$probs
```
:::

::: {.column width="35%"}
![](plot77.png) 

:::
:::
    
## Identifying and Validating Latent Classes (2)    
::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="33%"}
![](plot11.png)
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
![](plot22.png)
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
![](plot33.png)
:::

:::
## Identifying and Validating Latent Classes (3)
- [Model Adequacy & Stability]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}:
    - Why validation is needed:
        - To ensure that the classes reflect real alcohol-use patterns, not just numerical fit.
    - Validation Steps:
        - [Posterior probability accuracy]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}: High certainty for class membership.
     - [Outcome]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}: Stable, well-separated, and behaviorally meaningful latent classes.
     
::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
mean_posteriors
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](plot66.png)
      
:::
:::

## Discussion

- [K-Medoids vs LCA]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}

- [Next Steps]{style="color:#006400; font-weight:bold;"}:
    
    - Plot and visualize the latent classes
    - Perform inference on class differences
    - Investigate how individuals differ across clusters
